1: Amaziah son of Joash begins to reign as king of Judah in the second year of Jehoash son of Jehoahaz of Israel. The synchronism links the Judahite Joash's son to the Israelite Jehoash's second year — a dense chronological web.
2: Amaziah is twenty-five at accession and reigns twenty-nine years. His mother Jehoaddan is from Jerusalem — a purely Judahite lineage, unlike some queens with foreign connections. The maternal data grounds the king in the Jerusalem community.
3: wayyaʿaś hayyāšār bəʿênê YHWH ʾaḵ lōʾ kəḏāwiḏ ʾāḇîw kəkōl ʾăšer-ʿāśāh Yôʾāš ʾāḇîw ʿāśāh — 'he did right in YHWH's eyes, but not like David his father; according to all that Joash his father had done, he did.' The double qualification: he was better than some but the Davidic standard is still the ceiling he does not reach. His father Joash is the reference-point for his practice, not the ideal David.
4: The bāmôt formula again: high places not removed; the people still sacrifice and burn incense at the high places. Amaziah's otherwise-positive reign is limited by the decentralized cult that only Josiah will finally address.
5: Once Amaziah's kingdom was firmly in hand (kahăzaqāh hamamlākāh bəyāḏô), he struck down the servants who had killed his father the king. Political consolidation precedes the act of justice — the sequence is deliberate; Amaziah waits until his position is secure before acting against the assassins.
6: wəʾeṯ-bənê hammaккîm lōʾ hēmîṯ kikāṯûḇ bəsēper ṯôraṯ-môšeh ʾăšer-ṣiwwāh YHWH lēʾmōr lōʾ-yûmtû ʾāḇôṯ ʿal-bānîm ûḇānîm lōʾ-yûmtû ʿal-ʾāḇôṯ kî ʾim-ʾîš bəḥeṭʾô yûmāṯ — 'but the sons of the killers he did not put to death, as written in the Book of the Law of Moses, which YHWH commanded: fathers shall not be put to death for sons, nor sons for fathers; each for his own sin shall he be put to death' (Deuteronomy 24:16). This is one of the most explicit Deuteronomic citations in Kings, naming sēper ṯôraṯ-môšeh as the canonical authority. Amaziah applies individual-guilt doctrine against collective-family punishment — a legal principle that resists the ancient Near Eastern norm of clan accountability.
7: Amaziah defeats ten thousand Edomites in the Valley of Salt (gê-hammelaḥ) and takes Sela (haSSəlaʿ = the rock/cliff), renaming it Joktheel (yoqṯəʾēl). The Valley of Salt links to David's victories in the same locale (2 Samuel 8:13); Amaziah's Edom campaign is a minor Davidic echo, though without the significance of the original.
8: Amaziah sends envoys to Jehoash of Israel: lēḵāh niṯrāʾeh pānîm — 'come, let us look each other in the face,' i.e., meet in battle. The challenge is voluntary military aggression after the Edomite victory — success leading to overreach, a pattern the thistle-cedar parable in v9 directly names.
9: Jehoash sends back the thistle-cedar parable: a thistle in Lebanon sent to a cedar of Lebanon saying 'give your daughter to my son as wife'; then a wild beast passed by and trampled the thistle. The parable maps as: Amaziah = thistle, Jehoash = cedar; the wild beast = military reality that will crush a small king who overestimates himself. The imagery is from the genre of royal beast-fable; Jehoash deploys it as a dismissal and a warning.
10: hiḵêṯā ʾeṯ ʾĕḏôm ûnsāʾăḵā libbεḵā — 'you have defeated Edom and your heart has lifted you up.' nāśāʾ lēḇāḇ = pride/arrogance, the standard Hebrew idiom for hubris; military success has inflated Amaziah's self-assessment. Jehoash's advice: stay home and glory in your victory — why court disaster?
11: Amaziah does not listen (lōʾ šāmaʿ). The refusal to heed prophetic-parabolic warning is the pivot of the narrative; what follows is judgment on the refusal. Jehoash and Amaziah face each other at Beth-shemesh in Judah — the battle is in Judahite territory, an invasion of the south.
12: Judah is defeated before Israel; every man flees to his home. wayyinnāgep Yəhûḏāh — Judah is struck/routed. The defeat is total and humiliating; a Judahite king whose overconfidence led him to invade Israel is beaten on his own soil.
13: Jehoash captures Amaziah at Beth-shemesh and brings him to Jerusalem. He then tears down the Jerusalem wall from the Gate of Ephraim to the Corner Gate — four hundred cubits of the city wall — breaching the city's defenses. The physical breach enacts the humiliation; Amaziah's overconfidence ends in his capital's wall destroyed.
14: Jehoash takes all the gold and silver and all the vessels found in YHWH's house and the palace, plus hostages, and returns to Samaria. The looting parallels Shishak's plunder under Rehoboam (1 Kings 14:25-26) and Hazael's extortion under Joash (2 Kings 12:18) — the temple and palace assets circulate between kingdoms as military spoils.
15: The rest of Jehoash's acts — his might (gəḇûrāṯô) and his war with Amaziah — are in the chronicles of the kings of Israel. This is the second archival notice for Jehoash (the first in 2 Kings 13:12), forming brackets around the Elisha deathbed and the Amaziah battle sections.
16: Jehoash dies and is buried in Samaria with the kings of Israel; Jeroboam II his son reigns in his place. The short notice launches the most economically and militarily successful reign in Israel's history.
17: Amaziah outlives Jehoash by fifteen years — he reigns twenty-nine years total, the last fifteen as a defeated, partially humiliated king. The text notes his survival after Jehoash's death without suggesting rehabilitation; the defeat defined the rest of his reign.
18: The rest of Amaziah's acts are in the Book of the Chronicles of the Kings of Judah — the archival reference for Judah's kings, parallel to Israel's chronicle.
19: A conspiracy against Amaziah arises in Jerusalem; he flees to Lachish (lāḵîš), the fortified Judahite city in the Shephelah. They send after him to Lachish and kill him there. The king who began by lawfully punishing his father's assassins ends by being assassinated himself — an ironic structural closure on the Joash-Amaziah regnal pair.
20: They bring his body on horses and bury him with his fathers in Jerusalem in the City of David. The burial honors the Davidic dynastic convention; despite assassination, he receives royal burial.
21: All the people of Judah (ʿam Yəhûḏāh) take Azariah (Uzziah), sixteen years old, and make him king in place of his father Amaziah. The people's role in the accession is unusual — normally succession is automatic; the people's active choice suggests a period of instability after the assassination.
22: Azariah built Elath (ʾêlaṯ) and restored it to Judah — probably the Red Sea port (Ezion-geber area) that gives access to trade routes; this brief note of building and restoration is one of the more positive administrative achievements attributed to any Judahite king in this period.
23: Jeroboam II son of Jehoash begins to reign over Israel in Samaria in the fifteenth year of Amaziah of Judah. His forty-one year reign (the longest of any northern king) makes him the most durable ruler of the Jehu dynasty and of the northern kingdom altogether.
24: wayyaʿaś hārāʿ bəʿênê YHWH lōʾ-sār mikkol-ḥaṭṭôʾṯ Yārābəʿām — the standard northern-kingdom indictment despite Jeroboam II's extraordinary political and military success. The Deuteronomic historian evaluates by covenant faithfulness, not by political achievement; the most successful northern king still receives the lowest theological grade.
25: hûʾ hēšîḇ ʾeṯ-gəḇûl yiśrāʾēl mimmābôʾ ḥămaṯ ʿaḏ-yām hāʿărāḇāh kidbar YHWH ʾĕlōhê yiśrāʾēl ʾăšer dibber bəyaḏ-ʿaḇḏô Yônāh ḇen-ʾĂmiṯṯay hannāḇîʾ — 'he restored the border of Israel from Lebo-hamath to the Sea of the Arabah, according to the word of YHWH the God of Israel, which he spoke by his servant Jonah son of Amittai, the prophet.' This is the only mention of Jonah ben Amittai in Kings; he is identified as a prophet who proclaimed Israel's territorial restoration — a very different commission from the Nineveh mission of the book named for him. ʿeḇeḏ YHWH (servant of YHWH) here applied to Jonah is the same designation as the Isaianic Servant Songs; Jonah is simultaneously a historical prophet and an ironic figure whose own book records his reluctance to deliver YHWH's word.
26: ʾêyn ʿōṣēr wəʾêyn ʿāzēr lə yiśrāʾēl — 'there was no one restraining and no one helping for Israel'; the nation was in its most desperate condition. YHWH saw Israel's exceedingly bitter affliction (mar məʾōḏ) and that there was none — literally, no deliverer. The divine seeing and responding to communal desperate suffering underlies the restoration through Jeroboam II.
27: wəlōʾ-ḏibber YHWH lǝmāḥôṯ ʾeṯ-šēm yiśrāʾēl mittaḥaṯ haššāmayim — 'and YHWH had not spoken to blot out the name of Israel from under heaven.' The Deuteronomic covenant includes total covenant dissolution as the ultimate sanction; YHWH's restraint from enacting this — despite continued Jeroboam-pattern sins — is attributed to his compassion (v26). YHWH saves Israel through Jeroboam II not because of the king's righteousness but because of his own prior commitment not to blot out the name.
28: The rest of Jeroboam II's acts — his might (gəḇûrāṯô), his wars, his restoration of Damascus and Hamath to Judah (reading with LXX) — are in the chronicles of the kings of Israel. The extraordinary scope of his campaigns is noted and then referenced to the archive; the historian is more interested in covenant evaluation than in military geography.
29: Jeroboam II dies and is buried in Samaria with the kings of Israel; his son Zechariah reigns in his place. The single succession line that ends four generations later in Zechariah's assassination (2 Kings 15:10) closes the Jehu dynasty — the longest-ruling northern dynasty ends with the dynasty's predicted limit (2 Kings 10:30: four generations on the throne).
